meta.title: Russian L1 B2 Foundation Diagnostic
meta.author: Claude Code (CLI session)
meta.purpose: Outside-in discovery instrument for hidden Russian-L1 B2 foundation gaps. Items sourced from contrastive linguistics catalogues (Swan & Smith Russian chapter, Ionin Fluctuation Hypothesis, Slabakova aspect work, EFCAMDAT/CLC L1=ru B2 error tags), not from coach_notes.weak_patterns. The goal is to find what isn't yet tracked.
instructions_for_test_taker: Single answer per item; no retries.
No in-session feedback - scoring is offline after the session ends.
Items mixed across clusters - back-to-back items will not telegraph what is being tested.
Skip the item by typing PASS if you genuinely have no candidate answer. PASS is data (avoidance signal), not failure.
For RU→EN items: aim for a natural English rendering, not literal word-by-word. One sentence per stimulus unless the stimulus is multi-clause.
For free-write prompts: 3-5 sentences max; use the prompted register and theme.
instructions_for_scorer: Score per-item as correct / partial / incorrect.
Tag every incorrect or partial response with the matching l1_trap_id (or 'other' if the error doesn't match the catalogued trap).
Compute per-cluster correctness and surface (recognition vs production) breakdown.
Flag recognition-passes / production-fails delta per cluster - this is the avoidance signature.

clusters.articles: Russian has no article system. Tests definiteness/specificity grid + generic mass/plural + post-modifier definites.
clusters.prepositions: Verb + preposition collocations where Russian case government doesn't transfer.
clusters.tense_aspect: Russian pf/ipf binary doesn't map to English perfect/progressive. Targets present perfect, past perfect, sequence of tenses.
clusters.conditionals: Russian single conditional (бы + past) maps to four+ English patterns.
clusters.modals: Russian должен maps to must/have to/should; no past-modal + perfect-infinitive construction.
clusters.verb_complementation: Closed-set gerund vs infinitive licensors; Russian defaults to infinitive after most verbs.
clusters.word_order: Indirect question word order, do-support, adverb placement.
clusters.agreement: Collective + mass nouns + indefinite pronouns; existential there + plural.
clusters.pronouns: Expletive it/there absent in Russian; topic-comment cleft calques.
clusters.prep_gerund: Preposition + gerund where Russian licenses preposition + verbal noun in oblique case; L1 trap substitutes infinitive marker 'to'.
clusters.comparatives: than/that, double comparative, as...as, the more...the more, intensifier choice.

format_legend.gap: English stem with ___; learner selects from fixed options
format_legend.mcq: Choose the correct full sentence from options
format_legend.error_correction: Stem contains one L1-typical error; learner produces corrected sentence
format_legend.ru_to_en: Russian stimulus; learner produces English translation
format_legend.free_write_prompt: English prompt; learner produces 3-5 sentence constrained response

items.stem: ___ refinery upgrade that the board approved in February is now eight months behind schedule.
items.answer: The
items.explanation: Post-modified definite NP: the restrictive relative clause ('that the board approved in February') makes the NP [+definite +specific]. Russian uses bare noun for definiteness via word order; English requires 'the'.

items.stem: Coaches usually agree that ___ cyclists who train above 80 km a week need at least one full recovery day.
items.calque_distractor: the
items.explanation: Generic bare plural with restrictive relative clause is still generic ('the kind of cyclist who...'). English uses zero article for generic plurals; L1-ru trap overgeneralises 'the' from the relative-clause cue.

items.stem: I need to speak to ___ manager on duty tonight — whoever happens to be in.
items.answer: the
items.calque_distractor: a
items.explanation: Ionin Fluctuation Hypothesis cell [+definite −specific]. The role is unique and presupposed; the specific person is unknown to the speaker. English uses 'the'; L1-ru article-less learners fluctuate to 'a' when specificity is low.

items.stem: The success in our line of work depends on relationships, not on credentials alone.
items.answer: Success in our line of work depends on relationships, not on credentials alone.
items.explanation: Generic abstract noun; takes zero article. Russian uses bare noun for generics, but L1-ru learners over-correct to 'the' when they detect any postmodifier ('in our line of work'). Compare 'the success of the merger' (specific) vs 'success' (generic).

items.stem: Дом, в котором мы живём вот уже три года, принадлежит британскому экспату, который сдаёт его через агента.
items.answer: The house we have been living in for three years (now) belongs to a British expat who rents it (out) through an agent.|The house we've been living in for three years belongs to a British expat who rents it through an agent.
items.calque_distractor: House we live three years in it belongs to British expat that rent it through agent.
items.explanation: Tests: (a) post-modified definite 'the house' (relative clause), (b) [−def +specific] 'a British expat', (c) post-modified [+def] 'the agent' or [−def] 'an agent' both acceptable. L1-ru trap drops 'the' on the relative-clause-modified subject.

items.stem: На прошлой неделе со мной связался один хедхантер с интересным предложением от ближневосточной нефтяной компании.
items.answer: Last week a head-hunter contacted me with an interesting offer from a Middle Eastern oil company.|Last week a headhunter reached out to me with an interesting offer from a Gulf oil company.
items.calque_distractor: Last week the headhunter contacted me with the interesting offer from the Middle Eastern oil company.
items.explanation: Cell [−def +specific] for all three NPs ('one specific headhunter', 'one specific offer', 'one specific company'). Russian один marks specificity; L1-ru Fluctuation predicts overuse of 'the' when specificity is high. English uses 'a/an'.

items.stem: Сон и восстановление важнее для прогресса, чем объём тренировок.
items.answer: Sleep and recovery matter more for progress than training volume does.|Sleep and recovery are more important for progress than training volume.
items.calque_distractor: The sleep and the recovery are more important for the progress than the training volume.
items.explanation: All NPs are generic mass / abstract: zero article in English. L1-ru trap inserts 'the' on each. 'For progress' / 'for the progress of athletes' — the latter is specific via PP modifier, the former is generic.

items.stem: Write 3-4 sentences describing a typical week at your current job. Cover: what you do most days (generic), one specific project you're handling right now (definite), and one type of meeting you usually dread (indefinite generic).
expected_features: Generic plural with zero article for typical activities ('I review reports / I run meetings')
Definite article 'the' for the named specific project introduced as new
Indefinite 'a' with generic class ('a status meeting / a steering committee')
items.calque_distractor: Overuse of 'the' across generics; omission of 'the' on the specific project NP after first mention
items.explanation: Free-write surface for full article system under production load. Score: count article-drops on first-mention specifics + count article-overuses on generics. Avoidance pattern shows up if the writer favours bare or unmodified NPs to dodge the choice.

items.stem: The final approval depends ___ the board, not on the operations team.
items.answer: on
items.calque_distractor: from
items.explanation: English 'depend on'; Russian зависеть от ('from') is the canonical L1 calque trap. CLC tag RP (replacement preposition) high-frequency for L1-ru B2.

items.stem: I'll wait ___ you at the bike-hire place near the corniche entrance.
items.answer: for
items.explanation: English 'wait for someone'; Russian ждать takes direct genitive/accusative with no preposition. L1 trap omits 'for'. CLC tag MP.

items.stem: The current code-review workflow consists ___ three stages: lint, peer review, and a final QA pass.
items.answer: of
items.calque_distractor: from
items.explanation: English 'consist of'; Russian состоять из ('from') is the canonical calque. RP.

items.stem: We arrived to the office an hour late because the bridge was closed for maintenance.
items.answer: We arrived at the office an hour late because the bridge was closed for maintenance.
items.explanation: English 'arrive at' (specific point) / 'arrive in' (large area like a city); Russian прибыть в/на + acc calques to 'to'. RP, very high frequency for L1-ru.

items.stem: Мы вряд ли преуспеем в этом тендере, если не усилим коммерческую часть.
items.answer: We're unlikely to succeed in this tender unless we strengthen the commercial side.|We are unlikely to succeed in this bid if we don't strengthen the commercial part.
items.calque_distractor: We hardly will succeed at this tender if we will not strengthen commercial part.
items.explanation: Tests: 'succeed in' (not 'succeed at'); 'the commercial side/part' (postmod definite); first-conditional structure with negated antecedent. Russian преуспевать в maps to 'in' but L1 surface often outputs 'at'.

items.stem: Я извинился перед соседом за шум, который мы устроили в выходные.
items.answer: I apologised to the neighbour for the noise we made at the weekend.|I apologized to my neighbor for the noise we made over the weekend.
items.calque_distractor: I apologised before the neighbour for the noise that we made on the weekend.
items.explanation: Two prepositions in one item: 'apologise to [person]' + 'apologise for [thing]'. Russian извиниться перед ('before') + за ('for'). L1 trap calques 'перед' to 'before' or 'in front of'.

items.stem: Когда я езжу на длинные дистанции, я обычно слушаю подкасты про экономику.
items.answer: When I do long rides, I usually listen to podcasts about economics.|When I go on long rides I tend to listen to podcasts on economics.
items.calque_distractor: When I ride long distances I usually listen podcasts about economy.
items.explanation: English 'listen to' (preposition obligatory); Russian слушать takes direct accusative (no preposition). High-frequency L1-ru MP error. Also tests article on 'economics' (zero, generic abstract) and 'long rides' (zero, generic plural).

items.stem: Команда согласилась со мной по вопросу приоритетов, но мы всё ещё не договорились о сроках.
items.answer: The team agreed with me on the priorities, but we still haven't agreed on the timeline.|The team agreed with me about the priorities, but we still haven't agreed on a timeline.
items.calque_distractor: Team agreed with me by question of priorities, but we still didn't agree about deadlines.
items.explanation: Tests three 'agree' preposition uses: 'agree with [person]', 'agree on [matter]', 'agree to [proposal]'. Russian согласиться с covers 'with', then по вопросу ('on the question of') often gets calqued to 'by' or 'about'.

items.stem: I ___ in the Gulf since 2019, which makes me one of the longer-tenured people on the project.
items.answer: have lived
items.calque_distractor: live
items.explanation: Present perfect for state continuing from past into present, signalled by 'since 2019'. Russian past tense is unmarked for current relevance; L1 trap uses present simple ('I live here since 2019') or past simple.

items.stem: Which is the most natural reported version of: "I'm tired and I need a break" (said yesterday by a colleague)?
options: He said he has been tired and needs a break.
He said he was tired and (he) needed a break.
He said that he tired and need a break.
He said he is tired and he needs a break.
items.answer: He said he was tired and (he) needed a break.
items.calque_distractor: He said he is tired and he needs a break.
items.explanation: Sequence-of-tenses backshift after past reporting verb: present → past. Russian does not backshift, so L1 calque keeps the present. (B) is the default L1-ru trap pattern.

items.stem: When I got to the cafe, the rest of the group already left without me.
items.answer: When I got to the cafe, the rest of the group had already left without me.
items.explanation: Past perfect for the earlier of two past events; Russian uses perfective past for both, no morphological distinction. L1 trap renders the earlier event as past simple.

items.stem: Мы работаем над этим контрактом уже восемь месяцев и всё ещё не видим финиша.
items.answer: We have been working on this contract for eight months (now) and (we) still can't see the finish line.|We've been working on this contract for eight months and we still don't see an end in sight.
items.calque_distractor: We work on this contract already eight months and still don't see the finish.
items.explanation: Present perfect continuous for ongoing action started in the past, signalled by 'уже восемь месяцев' (= 'for eight months now'). Russian present-tense + duration adverb is the L1 pattern; English requires perfect continuous.

items.stem: На вчерашней встрече Клод сказал, что этот подход работает лучше того, что мы пробовали на прошлой неделе.
items.answer: In yesterday's meeting Claude said that this approach worked better than what we had tried the week before.|At yesterday's meeting Claude said this approach was working better than the one we'd tried the previous week.
items.calque_distractor: On yesterday meeting Claude said that this approach works better than what we tried last week.
items.explanation: Backshift on both clauses: 'работает' → 'worked/was working' (present → past); 'пробовали' (earlier past) → 'had tried'. Also 'last week' → 'the week before / the previous week' in indirect speech.

items.stem: К тому моменту, как я вышел на старт, остальные уже проехали первые десять километров.
items.answer: By the time I made it to the start, the others had already done the first ten kilometres.|By the time I got to the start line, the rest of the group had already covered the first ten kilometres.
items.calque_distractor: By the moment I came to the start, the others already passed the first ten kilometres.
items.explanation: Past perfect for action completed before another past event; trigger 'by the time' + 'уже проехали'. Russian uses perfective past for both; English requires past perfect for the earlier.

items.stem: Отдел закупок только что подтвердил заказ, но я ещё не видел самого документа.
items.answer: Procurement have just confirmed the order, but I haven't seen the actual document yet.|Procurement has just confirmed the order, but I still haven't seen the document itself.
items.calque_distractor: Procurement just confirmed the order, but I still didn't see the document.
items.explanation: Two present-perfect triggers: 'только что' → 'just', 'ещё не' → 'haven't ... yet'. Russian past tense is the L1 pattern; English requires present perfect for both. Also: 'procurement' takes singular or plural verb in BrE.

items.stem: If the regulator ___ a stricter local-content threshold tomorrow, half of our shortlist of contractors would no longer qualify.
items.answer: introduced
items.calque_distractor: would introduce
items.explanation: Type 2 conditional (hypothetical present/future): past simple in if-clause, would + bare infinitive in result. L1 trap puts 'would' in both clauses because Russian uses single 'бы + past' form throughout.

items.stem: Which is the natural counterfactual past form?
options: If I checked the weather forecast, I would not go out on that ride.
If I had checked the weather forecast, I would not go out on that ride.
If I would have checked the weather forecast, I would not have gone out on that ride.
If I had checked the weather forecast, I would not have gone out on that ride.
items.answer: If I had checked the weather forecast, I would not have gone out on that ride.
items.calque_distractor: If I would have checked the weather forecast, I would not have gone out on that ride.
items.explanation: Type 3 (counterfactual past): had + past participle in if-clause, would have + past participle in result. L1 trap (C) puts 'would have' in both clauses. (D) is mixed conditional (past condition → present/timeless result) and only works in certain readings.

items.stem: If the CFO would approve the budget tomorrow, we could start hiring next week.
items.answer: If the CFO approved the budget tomorrow, we could start hiring next week.
items.explanation: Hypothetical present: 'would' belongs in the result clause only. Russian бы appears in both clauses, so L1 trap surfaces 'would' twice.

items.stem: Если бы я выучил арабский в первый год, мне было бы сейчас гораздо проще решать бытовые вопросы.
items.answer: If I had learnt Arabic in my first year (here), everyday admin would be much easier (for me) now.|Had I learnt Arabic in my first year, dealing with day-to-day things would be much easier now.
items.calque_distractor: If I would learn Arabic in first year, it would be much easier now for me to solve household questions.
items.explanation: Mixed conditional: past hypothetical condition (had + past participle) → present consequence (would + bare infinitive). Russian's single бы form doesn't distinguish, so L1 production collapses to one of the canonical types (usually 2 or 3 uniformly).

items.stem: Если бы мы приняли их условия в январе, контракт бы уже действовал.
items.answer: If we had accepted their terms in January, the contract would already be in force (by now).|Had we accepted their terms back in January, the contract would have been live (running) by now.
items.calque_distractor: If we would accept their conditions in January, the contract would already work now.
items.explanation: Mixed conditional cousin to item 004: past condition → present consequence ('would already be in force'). Could also be rendered as pure type 3 with 'would already have been in force', both valid; pure mixed is the more natural register here.

items.stem: Если идёт сильный ветер с пустыни, я переношу тренировку на вечер. Если завтра будет такая же погода, я выеду рано утром.
items.answer: If there's a strong wind from the desert, I move my training to the evening. If the weather is the same tomorrow, I'll go out early in the morning.|If there's strong wind off the desert, I shift my ride to the evening. If we have the same weather tomorrow, I'll head out early in the morning.
items.calque_distractor: If strong wind goes from desert, I will move training on evening. If tomorrow will be same weather, I will go early morning.
items.explanation: Two conditionals in one stimulus: (1) habitual/zero conditional with present simple in both clauses, (2) type 1 future-real with present simple in if-clause and 'will' in result. L1 traps: 'will' in if-clause for type 1; 'will' in result of zero conditional.

items.stem: Context: nobody's heard from the supplier all afternoon. Which speculation is correct?
options: They must have lost network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
They must lose network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
They had to lose network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
They must to have lost network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
items.answer: They must have lost network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
items.calque_distractor: They must lose network coverage — the whole industrial area was down at three.
items.explanation: Modal + perfect infinitive ('must have + past participle') for past speculation/deduction. Russian has no parallel construction (uses adverbs like 'наверное' + past). L1 trap (B) drops 'have'; (C) adds 'to'.

items.stem: Looking back, I ___ the lease earlier — prices in this area have gone up nearly thirty percent since I signed it.
items.answer: should have renewed
items.calque_distractor: should renew
items.explanation: Past advice / regret: 'should have + past participle'. Russian uses 'нужно было' + infinitive; L1 trap maps to 'should + bare inf' (works for present advice, not past).

items.stem: Он, должно быть, уже ушёл домой — его машины нет на парковке.
items.answer: He must have gone home (already) — his car isn't in the car park.|He must have left for home — his car's not in the parking.
items.calque_distractor: He must already to go home — his car is not on the parking.
items.explanation: 'должно быть' + past = 'must have + past participle'. L1 trap is either 'must already left' or 'must to have gone'.

items.stem: Из-за новых правил клуба мы теперь должны регистрироваться на групповые выезды за день.
items.answer: Because of the new club rules we now have to sign up for group rides a day in advance.|With the new club regulations, we have to register for group rides one day ahead.
items.calque_distractor: Because of new rules of the club we must to register on group rides for one day.
items.explanation: External obligation (imposed by rules) → 'have to', not 'must'. Russian 'должны' is uniform for both; L1-ru default surface form is 'must' (sometimes 'must to').

items.stem: Тебе не обязательно писать всё в одном сообщении — можешь разбить на несколько.
items.answer: You don't have to put everything into one message — you can split it into several.|You don't need to write everything in a single message — you can break it up.
items.calque_distractor: You mustn't write all in one message — you can split on several.
items.explanation: Absence of obligation (= 'not necessary') → 'don't have to' / 'don't need to'. 'Mustn't' = prohibition (you are not allowed to), an entirely different meaning. Russian 'не обязательно' calques carelessly to 'mustn't'.

items.stem: The consultants suggested ___ the kick-off until after the audit, which makes sense given how fragile the numbers still look.
items.answer: postponing
items.calque_distractor: to postpone
items.explanation: 'Suggest' takes gerund (or 'that' + subjunctive). Russian предложить + infinitive is the canonical L1 trap. CLC tag FV.

items.stem: Most experienced riders here avoid ___ during the midday hours from May to September.
items.answer: training
items.calque_distractor: to train
items.explanation: 'Avoid' is gerund-only. Russian избегать + genitive of verbal noun maps either to gerund or (incorrectly) to infinitive.

items.stem: I actually enjoy ___ alongside an AI tool — the iteration loop is much faster than working alone.
items.answer: working
items.calque_distractor: to work
items.explanation: 'Enjoy' is gerund-only. Russian нравится + infinitive is the dominant L1 surface form (мне нравится работать).

items.stem: I would recommend to wait until Q3 before re-opening the question with the regulator.
items.answer: I would recommend waiting until Q3 before re-opening the question with the regulator.|I would recommend that we wait until Q3 before re-opening the question with the regulator.
items.explanation: 'Recommend' takes gerund or 'that' + subjunctive; never bare infinitive with 'to'. Russian рекомендовать + infinitive is the L1 calque trap.

items.stem: Несмотря на задержки, команда сумела закрыть квартал без существенных корректировок.
items.answer: Despite the delays, the team managed to close the quarter without any significant adjustments.|In spite of the delays, the team managed to close out the quarter with no material restatements.
items.calque_distractor: Despite delays, the team managed closing the quarter without significant corrections.
items.explanation: 'Manage' = succeed in doing, takes infinitive ('manage to do'). Russian суметь + infinitive maps cleanly here, but 'manage doing' is a high-frequency L1 over-generalisation by analogy with 'avoid/enjoy + ger'.

items.stem: Я стараюсь практиковать вход в повороты на высокой скорости по пятницам.
items.answer: I try to practise taking corners at speed on Fridays.|I'm trying to practise cornering at high speed on Fridays.
items.calque_distractor: I try practice to enter into turns on high speed on Friday.
items.explanation: 'Practise' is gerund-only. Russian практиковать + infinitive is the L1 trap. Also tests 'try + infinitive' (attempt) vs 'try + gerund' (experiment with) — here attempt is intended.

items.stem: По дороге к офису я остановился, чтобы перечитать их последнее письмо ещё раз.
items.answer: On the way to the office I stopped to re-read their last email one more time.|On the way to the office, I stopped to read their latest message again.
items.calque_distractor: On the way to office I stopped re-reading their last letter one more time.
items.explanation: 'Stop + to-inf' = stop in order to do; 'stop + ger' = cease doing. Russian остановиться, чтобы + infinitive maps cleanly to 'to-inf' here. L1 trap is the wrong reading (cessation).

items.stem: Наш сосед помог нам собрать велосипед в выходные.
items.answer: Our neighbour helped us assemble the bike at the weekend.|Our neighbor helped us put the bike together over the weekend.
items.calque_distractor: Our neighbour helped to us collect bicycle in weekend.
items.explanation: 'Help [someone] [bare-inf]' is the dominant pattern; 'help + to-inf' is also acceptable but less natural in BrE. L1 trap inserts 'to' before the helper-recipient or after.

items.stem: I'd like to understand what is the real bottleneck on the procurement side before I escalate this.
items.answer: I'd like to understand what the real bottleneck on the procurement side is before I escalate this.|I'd like to understand what the real bottleneck on the procurement side actually is before I escalate this.
items.explanation: Indirect (embedded) question → statement word order; no auxiliary inversion. Russian doesn't invert in direct questions either, so the L1 surface keeps SVO; but English-school 'inversion in questions' rule is then over-applied to embedded clauses.

items.stem: Which adverb placement is most natural in British/professional written English?
options: I start my day always with a quick review of overnight comments.
I always start my day with a quick review of overnight comments.
I start always my day with a quick review of overnight comments.
Always I start my day with a quick review of overnight comments.
items.answer: I always start my day with a quick review of overnight comments.
items.calque_distractor: Always I start my day with a quick review of overnight comments.
items.explanation: Frequency adverbs (always/often/usually) go between subject and main verb (or after auxiliary). Russian places всегда freely; L1 calques to sentence-initial or post-verbal.

items.stem: Where ___ usually buy groceries that aren't available at Lulu?
items.answer: do you
items.calque_distractor: you
items.explanation: Do-support obligatory in present-simple questions. Russian has no do-support; L1 trap omits ('Where you buy?'). Residual at B2 even when CC writing is otherwise clean.

items.stem: Никто в команде толком не знает, почему они снизили цену в последний момент.
items.answer: Nobody on the team really knows why they dropped the price at the last minute.|None of us on the team actually knows why they cut the price at the very last moment.
items.calque_distractor: Nobody in the team really knows why did they drop the price in the last moment.
items.explanation: Embedded wh-clause: 'почему они снизили' → 'why they dropped' (no 'did'). L1 over-applies do-support inversion to embedded clauses.

items.stem: По выходным я обычно выезжаю до восхода солнца, чтобы избежать жары.
items.answer: At the weekend I usually head out before sunrise to avoid the heat.|On weekends I'll normally set off before sunrise to avoid the heat.
items.calque_distractor: On weekends I go out usually before sunrise to avoid the heat.
items.explanation: 'Usually' goes after subject + before main verb. L1 calque places it after main verb. Also tests 'avoid + gerund' (cluster 6 overlap) and 'at the weekend' (BrE) vs 'on weekends' (AmE).

items.stem: Пришлите мне пожалуйста этот отчёт в PDF, и сбросьте его в общий чат.
items.answer: Please send me that report as a PDF, and drop it (in)to the team chat.|Could you send me that report as a PDF and drop it in the team channel, please.
items.calque_distractor: Please send me this report in PDF and drop in the common chat it.
items.explanation: Separable phrasal verbs require pronoun object BETWEEN verb and particle: 'drop it in', not 'drop in it'. Russian flexible word order doesn't preserve this constraint.

items.stem: There aren't ___ tier-one EPC contractors with the capacity to handle a project of this scale in the Gulf.
items.answer: many
items.calque_distractor: much
items.explanation: Count noun ('contractors') → many. Russian много is count-mass-blind; L1 trap uses 'much' across the board. ID/Q tag.

items.stem: Which is correct?
options: We have very little time left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
We have very less time left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
We have very few time left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
We have very little times left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
items.answer: We have very little time left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
items.calque_distractor: We have very few time left if we want to ride before the heat builds up.
items.explanation: 'Time' as mass noun → little (not few). Russian мало is blind to count/mass. (D) is the over-generalisation of comparative 'less' as a quantifier.

items.stem: I don't think there are some good arguments for changing the approach right now.
items.answer: I don't think there are any good arguments for changing the approach right now.
items.explanation: Within a negated clause, 'any' is the default polarity item; 'some' under negation is acceptable in offers/requests but signals presupposition the speaker doesn't intend here. Russian некоторые maps loosely; L1 default surface is 'some'.

items.stem: Слишком много людей в этом проекте и слишком мало времени, чтобы всё это прокоординировать.
items.answer: Too many people on this project and too little time to coordinate it all.|There are too many people on this project and far too little time to coordinate everything.
items.calque_distractor: Too much people on this project and too few time to coordinate all this.
items.explanation: 'Много людей' → 'many people' (count); 'мало времени' → 'little time' (mass). L1 trap reverses or homogenises.

items.stem: У нас есть некоторые хорошие рестораны в районе, но очень мало хороших кафе с кофе третьей волны.
items.answer: We have a few decent restaurants in the area, but very few good third-wave coffee shops.|There are a few decent restaurants around, but very few proper third-wave coffee shops.
items.calque_distractor: We have few good restaurants in the area but very little good coffee shops with third wave coffee.
items.explanation: 'A few' (positive: 'some, a small number') vs 'few' (negative: 'not many, fewer than expected'). Russian некоторые = 'a few'; мало = 'few/little'. L1 trap drops the article on 'a few', flipping the polarity.

items.stem: Я не вижу никаких оснований откладывать это решение ещё на один квартал.
items.answer: I don't see any reason to put this decision off by another quarter.|I see no reason to delay this decision by another quarter.
items.calque_distractor: I don't see any grounds to postpone this decision on one more quarter.
items.explanation: Negation → 'any' (not 'some'); alternative natural form: 'no reason' (raised negation). L1 default 'I don't see some reasons' carries Russian никаких too literally.

items.stem: The procurement team ___ still finalising the shortlist; we'll have an answer by Thursday.
items.answer: is
items.calque_distractor: are
items.explanation: Collective noun 'team' takes singular in formal/written English when treated as a unit (especially with progressive aspect referring to a single coordinated action). Both 'is' and 'are' are acceptable in BrE generally; here the discourse frame ('the team' as a single decision-maker) favours singular. Either should be accepted; scoring focuses on consistency.

items.stem: Could you share any informations you have on how other teams are using the assistant for similar workflows?
items.answer: Could you share any information you have on how other teams are using the assistant for similar workflows?
items.explanation: 'Information' is uncountable in English; no plural. Russian информация also has no plural, but L1-ru learners overgeneralise the English -s after seeing 'data', 'advice', 'equipment', 'feedback' across L2 input.

items.stem: Which is correct?
options: Everyone in the Saturday group is supposed to bring his own spare tube.
All in the Saturday group is supposed to bring their own spare tube.
Everyone in the Saturday group is supposed to bring their own spare tube.
Everyone in the Saturday group are supposed to bring their own spare tube.
items.answer: Everyone in the Saturday group is supposed to bring their own spare tube.
items.calque_distractor: Everyone in the Saturday group are supposed to bring their own spare tube.
items.explanation: Indefinite pronouns (everyone/somebody/nobody) → singular verb + singular 'their' (modern usage). Russian все is plural; L1 trap uses plural agreement. (C) is grammatically OK but uses gendered 'his' — less natural in modern usage.

items.stem: Нам нужны более свежие данные и лучшие советы по регуляторным вопросам, прежде чем мы выйдем на регулятора.
items.answer: We need fresher data and better advice on the regulatory side before we approach the regulator.|We need more current data and better guidance on regulatory questions before we go to the regulator.
items.calque_distractor: We need more fresh datas and better advices on regulatory questions before we will go to regulator.
items.explanation: 'Data' (treated as mass in modern usage; plural-with-singular-verb in formal) and 'advice' (always uncountable, no plural). L1-ru trap pluralises both.

items.stem: В нашем районе много семей, которые приехали в Бахрейн ради работы, а остались ради школ.
items.answer: There are a lot of families in our area who came to Bahrain for work and stayed for the schools.|In our neighbourhood there are many families who moved to Bahrain for the job and ended up staying for the schools.
items.calque_distractor: In our area is many families who came to Bahrain for work but stayed for schools.
items.explanation: Existential 'there are + plural'. Russian uses 'есть' (singular) or omits the copula entirely with locative inversion ('в районе ...'). L1 traps: 'is' with plural, or no expletive at all.

items.stem: By the time I left for work ___ was already 38 outside and the air had that thick pre-summer feel.
items.answer: it
items.explanation: Expletive 'it' for weather/time/distance. Russian было is impersonal with no subject ('было 38 градусов'). L1 trap omits the dummy subject.

items.stem: On the seventh floor ___ is a small meeting room nobody seems to know about — perfect for one-on-ones.
items.answer: there
items.explanation: Existential 'there + be' for first-mention of an entity at a location. Russian uses locative inversion + есть or just bare locative ('на седьмом этаже — есть / маленькая комната'). L1 trap omits 'there'.

items.stem: This is interesting question, but I don't think we have the bandwidth to take it on this quarter.
items.answer: That's an interesting question, but I don't think we have the bandwidth to take it on this quarter.|This is an interesting question, but I don't think we have the bandwidth to take it on this quarter.
items.explanation: Russian 'Это интересный вопрос' calques to English without the indefinite article. Two L1 errors fused: missing 'an' + 'this' where 'that' is more natural for referring to something just said.

items.stem: Сегодня утром было прохладно и свежо — идеальная погода для длинной тренировки.
items.answer: It was cool and fresh this morning — perfect weather for a long ride.|This morning was cool and crisp — ideal conditions for a long training ride.
items.calque_distractor: This morning was cool and fresh — ideal weather for long training.
items.explanation: Impersonal weather statement requires expletive 'it'. The alternative ('This morning was cool') uses 'morning' as the subject and is also fine. L1 default drops the subject entirely ('Was cool this morning').

items.stem: Именно отдел финансов, а не боард, заблокировал эту инициативу в прошлом квартале.
items.answer: It was the finance department, not the board, that blocked this initiative last quarter.|It was finance, not the board, that killed the initiative last quarter.
items.calque_distractor: Exactly finance department, not the board, blocked this initiative in the last quarter.
items.explanation: Emphatic 'it-cleft' for focus. Russian именно + topic-comment order doesn't have a structural English analogue; L1 calque is 'Exactly the finance...' or 'Just the finance...'. The cleft construction is a B2-target structural form.

items.stem: For the next two weeks I want the team to focus ___ getting the lender presentations watertight before we move on to anything else.
items.answer: on
items.calque_distractor: to
items.explanation: Pattern: focus on + gerund. Russian сосредоточиться на + locative; L1 calques 'on' but high-frequency variant calques the infinitive marker ('focus to get'). This is the trap that surfaced 2026-05-17.

items.stem: Would the wider team be interested ___ trialling the new commit-message workflow on the staging branch first?
items.answer: in
items.calque_distractor: to
items.explanation: 'Be interested in + gerund'; Russian заинтересован в + locative also maps to 'in', but L1 surface frequently switches to 'to' by analogy with the infinitival sense of 'want/wish to'.

items.stem: I look forward to hear back from your team on the revised commercial terms.
items.answer: I look forward to hearing back from your team on the revised commercial terms.
items.explanation: 'Look forward to' is preposition + gerund; the 'to' is NOT the infinitive marker. Classic Russian-L1 trap because Russian ждать (which 'look forward to' often translates to) takes a noun, not a verb form; learner infers 'to' = infinitive marker.

items.stem: Нам удалось сократить время на согласование договоров почти вдвое без потери качества.
items.answer: We succeeded in cutting contract turnaround time almost in half without losing quality.|We managed to cut contract approval time by almost half without sacrificing quality.
items.calque_distractor: We succeeded to reduce time on contract approvals almost twice without quality loss.
items.explanation: 'Succeed in + gerund' (also 'manage to + inf' as a paraphrase). L1 trap is 'succeed to do' or 'succeed at doing'.

items.stem: Вместо того чтобы ехать длинную дистанцию в эту жару, я вчера сделал интервальную тренировку на станке.
items.answer: Instead of riding a long distance in this heat, I did intervals on the indoor trainer yesterday.|Instead of going long in this heat, I did an interval session on the turbo trainer yesterday.
items.calque_distractor: Instead to ride long distance in this heat, I made intervals on the bench yesterday.
items.explanation: 'Instead of + gerund'. Russian вместо того чтобы + infinitive is a very strong L1 trap because the subordinator 'чтобы' calques to the infinitive marker.

items.stem: Корпоративный IT настаивает на том, чтобы мы пропускали весь трафик через их VPN.
items.answer: Corporate IT insists on us routing all our traffic through their VPN.|Corporate IT insists that all our traffic go through their VPN.
items.calque_distractor: Corporate IT insists to make us pass all traffic through their VPN.
items.explanation: 'Insist on + (object) + gerund' or 'insist that + clause + subjunctive'. Russian настаивать на том, чтобы ... maps cleanly to 'on + gerund' but L1 trap calques 'чтобы' as 'to + inf'.

items.stem: Which sentence is correct?
options: The new local supplier is almost as competitive than the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
The new local supplier is almost as competitive, like the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
The new local supplier is almost so competitive as the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
The new local supplier is almost as competitive as the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
items.answer: The new local supplier is almost as competitive as the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
items.calque_distractor: The new local supplier is almost so competitive as the European one, but their lead times are still longer.
items.explanation: 'As ... as' is the standard equative comparative. Russian такой же ... как maps loosely; (D) 'столь ... сколь' style calque produces archaic 'so ... as'. (B) blends with 'than'.

items.stem: The new wheelset feels more lighter on climbs, and more responsive in the corners.
items.answer: The new wheelset feels lighter on climbs, and more responsive in the corners.
items.explanation: Double comparative: 'more' + comparative form is ungrammatical. Russian doesn't allow this either (*более лучше* is wrong), but L1-ru learners produce it in English under processing load.

items.stem: Их предложение не такое выгодное, как хотелось бы, но и не такое плохое, чтобы выходить из переговоров.
items.answer: Their offer isn't as attractive as we'd hoped, but it isn't bad enough to walk away from the talks.|Their proposal isn't quite as good as we wanted, but not bad enough to pull out of the negotiations.
items.calque_distractor: Their offer is not so beneficial as we wanted, but not so bad to leave the negotiations.
items.explanation: '(Not) as X as ...' + 'not X enough to ...' (result construction). L1 traps: 'so X as' archaic, 'not so bad to' instead of 'not bad enough to'.

items.stem: Чем раньше выезжаешь, тем меньше трафика вдоль корниша.
items.answer: The earlier you head out, the less traffic there is along the corniche.|The earlier you set off, the lighter the traffic is along the corniche.
items.calque_distractor: More earlier you go out, more less traffic is on corniche.
items.explanation: 'The + comparative ..., the + comparative ...' correlative construction. Russian чем ..., тем ... maps directly but L1-ru surface often produces 'more X, more Y' without 'the', or with double-comparative ('more earlier').

items.stem: Для совместной работы новая версия намного быстрее, хотя всё ещё раза в два дороже.
items.answer: For collaborative work the new version is much (a lot / far) faster, although it's still about twice as expensive.|For collaboration, the new version is significantly faster, though it still costs roughly twice as much.
items.calque_distractor: For joint work the new version is more faster, although still twice more expensive.
items.explanation: Intensifier with comparative: 'much/far/a lot/significantly + comparative'; never 'more + comparative'. Multiplicative comparison: 'twice as X as / twice the X'. L1 trap is double comparative 'more faster' + literal calque 'twice more expensive'.
